1: Then Darius the king made a decree, and search was made in Babylonia, in the house of the archives where documents were stored. The imperial archive search — looking for Cyrus's original decree. The record has been kept, though buried in the archives. The covenant's authorization is always recoverable because 'the grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever' (Isaiah 40:8). What YHWH decreed through Cyrus remains in the record.
2: And in Ecbatana, the citadel that is in the province of Media, a scroll was found on which this was written: 'A record.' The scroll found in the archives of Ecbatana — the covenant's prior authorization surfaces from the Median capital. Christ's own authorization was found 'in the scroll of the book' (Hebrews 10:7, citing Psalms 40:7): 'Behold, I have come to do your will, O God, as it is written of me.' The record was there all along; the search reveals it.
3: 'In the first year of Cyrus the king, Cyrus the king issued a decree: Concerning the house of God at Jerusalem, let the house be rebuilt, the place where sacrifices are offered, and let its foundations be retained.' The original decree preserved — the first year of Cyrus, the specific authorization for the foundation and dimensions. Isaiah's prophecy named Cyrus specifically (Isaiah 44:28); the found scroll confirms the prophecy's fulfillment. Christ is similarly grounded in prior authorization: 'all the prophets bore witness' (Acts 10:43).
4: 'Its height shall be sixty cubits and its breadth sixty cubits, with three layers of great stones and one layer of timber. Let the cost be paid from the royal treasury.' The specific dimensions and funding source — the empire paying for YHWH's house. The generosity of the imperial authorization is itself a covenant sign: 'the kings of the earth will bring their glory' into the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24). The empire's resources serving covenant purposes is the OT anticipation of the eschatological ingathering.
5: 'And also let the gold and silver vessels of the house of God, which Nebuchadnezzar took out of the temple that is in Jerusalem and brought to Babylon, be restored and brought back to the temple that is in Jerusalem.' The decree specifically orders the return of the temple vessels. The lost and the plundered will be returned — the covenant principle of restoration through justice. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate form of this return: what was taken by death (his life, his body) is restored by the Father's power.
6: 'Now therefore, Tattenai, governor of Beyond the River, Shethar-bozenai, and your associates the governors who are in Beyond the River, keep away from there.' The adversaries commanded to step back — the very officials who opposed the building are now ordered by imperial edict to stand down. The reversal of power is total: the accusers become the commanded bystanders. Christ promises the same reversal: 'those who humble themselves will be exalted' and 'the stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone' (Matthew 21:42).
7: 'Let the work on this house of God alone. Let the governor of the Jews and the elders of the Jews rebuild this house of God on its site.' The imperial permission transformed into imperial command to leave the builders in peace. The freedom to build granted from the highest earthly authority. Christ's commission comes from the highest divine authority: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore...' (Matthew 28:18-19) — the building commission backed by absolute authorization.
8: 'Moreover, I make a decree regarding what you shall do for these elders of the Jews for the rebuilding of this house of God. The cost is to be paid to these men in full and without delay from the royal revenue, the tribute of Beyond the River.' Imperial funding for the temple — the adversarial administration now required to fund the building project it tried to stop. The resources of the empire redirected to covenant purposes. Christ's kingdom similarly draws on the world's resources for its mission: 'the wealth of the nations shall come to you' (Isaiah 60:5).
9: 'And whatever is needed — bulls, rams, or sheep for burnt offerings to the God of heaven, wheat, salt, wine, or oil, as the priests at Jerusalem require — let that be given to them day by day without fail.' The empire providing the sacrificial materials — the daily provisions for temple worship funded from the imperial treasury. Christ provides the ultimate sacrificial supply: himself, once for all (Hebrews 10:10), so that no further daily provision for sacrifice is required. The temple economy dissolves into his single offering.
10: 'That they may offer pleasing sacrifices to the God of heaven and pray for the life of the king and his sons.' The king requesting prayer from the restored temple — the empire acknowledging that YHWH's favor mediated through the temple is valuable. Paul commands prayer for kings and all in authority (1 Timothy 2:1-2). The covenant community's prayer for political authorities is itself a form of covenant testimony to the one who holds all authority.
11: 'Also I make a decree that if anyone alters this edict, a beam shall be pulled from his house, and he shall be impaled on it, and his house shall be made a dunghill.' The severe curse on anyone who reverses the building authorization. The severity of the protection mirrors the severity of the opposition — empire inverted from threat to shield. Christ's promise to his church is similarly absolute: 'on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18).
12: 'May the God who has caused his name to dwell there overthrow any king or people who shall put out a hand to alter this, or to destroy this house of God that is in Jerusalem. I Darius make this decree; let it be done with all diligence.' The pagan king invoking YHWH's protection of his own house — the Gentile ruler acknowledging the God whose dwelling place he is protecting. The nations' acknowledgment of YHWH reaches its eschatological form in Revelation 21:24-26: 'the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it.'
13: Then, according to the word sent by Darius the king, Tattenai, the governor of Beyond the River, Shethar-bozenai, and their associates did with all diligence what Darius the king had ordered. The former adversaries now execute the king's commission with diligence — the complete reversal. The same officials who filed accusations against the builders now provision and protect the building. The reversal of the adversaries mirrors the cosmic reversal Christ achieves: 'having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15).
14: And the elders of the Jews built and prospered through the prophesying of Haggai the prophet and Zechariah the son of Iddo. They finished their building by decree of the God of Israel and by decree of Cyrus and Darius and Artaxerxes king of Persia. The building completed by the convergence of divine decree and imperial decree — YHWH's will executed through human authority. Christ's mission is similarly executed through the convergence of divine purpose and human agency: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' (Acts 2:23).
15: This house was finished on the third day of the month of Adar, in the sixth year of the reign of Darius the king. The third day — the temple completed on the third day of the month. The third-day completion is theologically resonant in the canonical narrative: Christ raises the true temple on the third day (John 2:19-21: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up'). The date is coincidental in the narrative; the pattern it embodies is not.
16: And the people of Israel, the priests and the Levites, and the rest of the returned exiles, celebrated the dedication of this house of God with joy. The great celebration of completion — the joy of the dedication after decades of struggle. The dedication of Solomon's temple was marked by the glory-cloud filling the house (2 Chronicles 7:1-3); this dedication is marked by joy of the returned exiles. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate dedication: 'the joy that was set before him' (Hebrews 12:2) is the eschatological joy behind which the dedication of the true temple stands.
17: They offered at the dedication of this house of God 100 bulls, 200 rams, 400 lambs, and as a sin offering for all Israel 12 male goats, according to the number of the tribes of Israel. The dedication offerings — generous but modest compared to Solomon's dedication (22,000 oxen, 120,000 sheep: 2 Chronicles 7:5). The diminished scale of the restoration mirrors the diminished glory Haggai describes (Haggai 2:3); yet Haggai promises the latter glory will exceed the former. Christ is that latter glory — 'something greater than the temple is here' (Matthew 12:6).
18: And they set the priests in their divisions and the Levites in their courses, for the service of God at Jerusalem, as it is written in the Book of Moses. The restoration of the organized worship-service — the Levitical divisions re-established according to the Torah. The church's organized ministry similarly traces to the appointment of the apostles, then deacons, then elders and overseers — ordered service in the covenant community as the pattern of worship-in-action.
19: On the fourteenth day of the first month, the returned exiles kept the Passover. The first Passover of the restoration period — the ultimate covenant meal of liberation re-enacted by those who have just experienced liberation from Babylon. Christ redefines the Passover at the Last Supper: 'This is my body given for you; do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19). The Passover kept by the returned exiles anticipates the Passover kept by those returned from the exile of sin and death.
20: For the priests and the Levites had purified themselves together; all of them were clean. So they slaughtered the Passover lamb for all the returned exiles, for their brothers the priests, and for themselves. All pure — the restoration community keeping the Passover with uniform ritual purity. The NT Passover is kept with the purity that Christ provides: 'you are already clean because of the word that I have spoken to you' (John 15:3). The purity for the feast is now a gift, not an achievement.
21: It was eaten by the people of Israel who had returned from exile, and also by everyone who had joined them and separated himself from the uncleanness of the peoples of the land to worship the LORD, the God of Israel. The inclusion of those who separated themselves from the nations' uncleanness — converts who joined the covenant community. The widened Passover table: 'those from the nations' joining the feast. Christ's Last Supper is similarly eschatologically widened: 'many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 8:11).
22: And they kept the Feast of Unleavened Bread seven days with joy, for the LORD had made them joyful and had turned the heart of the king of Assyria to them, so that he aided them in the work of the house of God, the God of Israel. YHWH turned the heart of the king — Darius of Persia called 'king of Assyria' because he now ruled the Assyrian territories. The divine sovereignty over royal hearts (Proverbs 21:1: 'The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the LORD') is the ground of the community's joy. Christ is Lord over all earthly authority: 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18).
